title: SPONGE Signed Spectral Clustering: Complete Implementation Specification
summary: Comprehensive implementation recipe for SPONGE signed spectral clustering on Co-Information feature graphs. Covers exact mathematical formulations for both SPONGE and SPONGE_sym generalized eigenvalue problems, SigNet library API (archived Nov 2025, recommend vendoring ~250 lines), eigensolver selection for dense 200-1000 node graphs (scipy.linalg.eigh for d<=500, eigsh shift-invert for d<=1000), spectral frustration index via lambda_min of signed Laplacian, eigengap+silhouette k-selection, Co-Information computation via sklearn KSG with custom joint MI implementation, complete 9-step pseudocode pipeline, scalability estimates, and seven documented pitfalls with mitigations.
answer:
## 1. Algorithm Overview

SPONGE (Signed Positive Over Negative Generalized Eigenproblem) is a spectral clustering algorithm for signed graphs introduced by Cucuringu et al. [1]. Unlike standard spectral clustering which only handles positive (similarity) edge weights, SPONGE explicitly models both positive edges (agreement/redundancy) and negative edges (disagreement/synergy) by decomposing the signed adjacency matrix into separate positive and negative components and solving a generalized eigenvalue problem [1]. The algorithm simultaneously maximizes within-cluster positive edges while maximizing between-cluster negative edges [1, 2]. Two variants exist: unnormalized SPONGE and normalized SPONGE_sym, with SPONGE_sym generally preferred for robustness across diverse settings including varying cluster sizes and sparse graphs [1, 2].

## 2. Mathematical Formulation

### 2.1 Signed Adjacency Decomposition

Given a signed weighted adjacency matrix A, decompose it into positive and negative components [1]:
- A_plus_ij = max(A_ij, 0) — positive entries only (redundancy edges in the Co-Information context)
- A_minus_ij = max(-A_ij, 0) — absolute value of negative entries (synergy edges)
- Identity: A = A_plus - A_minus [1]

### 2.2 Degree Matrices

- D_plus_ii = sum_j A_plus_ij — positive degree (sum of positive weights incident to node i) [1]
- D_minus_ii = sum_j A_minus_ij — negative degree (sum of absolute negative weights) [1]
- D_bar_ii = D_plus_ii + D_minus_ii = sum_j |A_ij| — total absolute degree [1, 14]

### 2.3 Graph Laplacians

- L_plus = D_plus - A_plus — standard Laplacian of positive subgraph [1]
- L_minus = D_minus - A_minus — standard Laplacian of negative subgraph [1]
- L_sym_plus = (D_plus)^{-1/2} L_plus (D_plus)^{-1/2} — normalized positive Laplacian [1, 6]
- L_sym_minus = (D_minus)^{-1/2} L_minus (D_minus)^{-1/2} — normalized negative Laplacian [1, 6]
- L_signed = D_bar - A — signed Laplacian used for frustration index computation [14, 24]

### 2.4 SPONGE Objective

The SPONGE objective minimizes the ratio of positive-cut to negative-cut across k clusters [1]:

  min sum_c [ x_c^T (L_plus + tau_neg * D_minus) x_c ] / [ x_c^T (L_minus + tau_pos * D_plus) x_c ]

The numerator penalizes cutting positive edges and rewards grouping negative-degree nodes; the denominator rewards cutting negative edges [1].

### 2.5 Generalized Eigenvalue Problems

- SPONGE: (L_plus + tau_neg * D_minus) w = lambda (L_minus + tau_pos * D_plus) w [1]
- SPONGE_sym: (L_sym_plus + tau_neg * I) w = lambda (L_sym_minus + tau_pos * I) w [1, 2]

The solution takes the k eigenvectors corresponding to the k smallest eigenvalues, forms an n x k matrix V, and applies k-means++ to the rows of V to obtain cluster assignments [1]. Notably, SigNet's implementation divides eigenvectors by eigenvalues (v = v / w) before applying k-means [6].

### 2.6 Regularization Parameters

The regularization parameters tau_plus and tau_minus default to 1.0 [1, 3]. They must be strictly positive to prevent singular matrices when some nodes have zero positive or zero negative degree [1]. The theoretical constraint for correct eigenvector ordering under the Signed Stochastic Block Model (SSBM) is: tau_minus < tau_plus * (n/2 - 1 + eta) / (n/2 - eta) [1]. For sparse graphs, adaptive scaling as gamma = (np)^{6/7} is recommended, where p is the edge probability [2].

### 2.7 Theoretical Guarantees

Under the SSBM, SPONGE recovers the true partition with high probability when p >= c1' * log(n)/n in the dense regime [1]. The sparse regime requires more stringent conditions: p >= C4^{14} / (delta^{28} * n) [2]. The aspect ratio constraint requires sqrt(rho) > 1 - 1/[4k(2+sqrt(k))] where rho = s/l (smallest/largest cluster fraction) [2].

## 3. SigNet Library Reference

The SigNet library (alan-turing-institute/SigNet) was archived on November 4, 2025 and is now read-only [5]. Its last significant commit dates to September 2018 [5]. The library has approximately 40 stars and 10 forks on GitHub [5].

### 3.1 Installation and Dependencies

SigNet can be installed via pip install git+https://github.com/alan-turing-institute/SigNet.git [5]. Its dependencies include scikit-learn, cvxpy, and networkx (none with version constraints) [7]. Critically, the cvxpy dependency is only required for the SDP_cluster method, not for SPONGE or SPONGE_sym [7]. No Python version is specified in setup.py, and the core code uses basic numpy/scipy/sklearn APIs that are compatible with Python 3.10/3.11 [7].

### 3.2 API

The Cluster class accepts a tuple of (A_pos, A_neg) as scipy.sparse.csc_matrix objects [3, 4]. The constructor computes several attributes including the positive adjacency p, negative adjacency n, signed adjacency A = p - n, positive degree diagonal D_p, negative degree diagonal D_n, total degree Dbar = D_p + D_n, and the symmetrically normalized adjacency normA [3, 6].

Key method signatures [3, 6]:
- SPONGE(k=4, tau_p=1, tau_n=1, eigens=None, mi=None) — eigens defaults to k-1 eigenvectors, mi defaults to matrix size for LOBPCG max iterations
- SPONGE_sym(k=4, tau_p=1, tau_n=1, eigens=None, mi=None) — uses normalized Laplacians with identity matrix regularization

The SPONGE implementation constructs matrix1 = (D_p - A_pos) + tau_n * D_n and matrix2 = (D_n - A_neg) + tau_p * D_p, then solves via lobpcg(matrix1, v0, B=matrix2, largest=False) [6]. Zero-degree node handling uses sqrtinvdiag() with the safeguard dd = 1/max(sqrt(x), 1/999999999) to prevent division by zero [8].

### 3.3 Recommendation

VENDOR the cluster.py (~200 lines) and utils.py (~50 lines) directly into the project [5, 6, 7, 8]. This avoids the cvxpy dependency (only needed for SDP_cluster, not SPONGE), ensures Python 3.10/3.11 compatibility, and allows replacing the LOBPCG eigensolver with dense eigensolvers appropriate for our matrix sizes [6, 7].

## 4. Eigensolver Selection for Dense Graphs (d=200-1000)

This is a CRITICAL implementation decision. For Co-Information feature graphs with d=200-1000 features, the adjacency matrix is fully dense because every pair of features has a computed Co-Information value. SigNet's default LOBPCG solver is inappropriate for these sizes [10].

### 4.1 LOBPCG Limitations

LOBPCG breaks internally when 5k > n [10]. For k=20 clusters with n=200 features, this constraint is violated. The solver has known convergence failures with provided initial guesses, producing 'leading minor not positive definite' errors (scipy issue #10258) [20]. LOBPCG is designed for extremely large n/k ratios and solves 3k x 3k dense eigenproblems per iteration, so performance degrades as k approaches n [10].

### 4.2 Solver Recommendations

For d <= 500: Use scipy.linalg.eigh(A, b=B, subset_by_index=[0, k-1]) — the direct dense solver is exact, numerically stable, and runs in O(n^3) time which is under 1 second for n=500 [9]. B must be positive DEFINITE (not just semidefinite) [9].

For 500 < d <= 1000: Use scipy.linalg.eigh (still fast at ~5-30 seconds) or scipy.sparse.linalg.eigsh with shift-invert mode sigma=0.0 [9, 11]. The eigsh solver allows B to be positive semidefinite when sigma is specified [11].

For d > 1000: Use scipy.sparse.linalg.lobpcg with sparse matrices and random initial vectors [10]. This is the only case where LOBPCG is appropriate.

### 4.3 B Matrix Positive Definiteness

scipy.linalg.eigh requires B to be positive definite [9]. The SPONGE B matrix is L_minus + tau_pos * D_plus. Since L_minus is positive semidefinite and tau_pos * D_plus adds positive diagonal entries from positive degrees, B is positive definite as long as every node has at least one positive edge [1, 9]. For safety, add epsilon * I with epsilon=1e-10 [9].

### 4.4 Generalized-to-Standard Conversion

If B is positive definite, the generalized problem Aw = lambda Bw can be converted to standard form: B^{-1/2} A B^{-1/2} z = lambda z, then w = B^{-1/2} z [9]. This can be done via Cholesky decomposition of B.

## 5. Spectral Frustration Index

### 5.1 Definition and Theory

The signed Laplacian is L(Sigma) = D_bar - A_signed, where D_bar_ii = sum_j |A_ij| and A_signed is the original signed adjacency matrix [14]. A fundamental result in signed graph theory states that a signed graph is balanced if and only if lambda_min(L(Sigma)) = 0 [14, 24]. When lambda_min > 0, it measures 'how far from balanced' the graph is — larger lambda_min means more frustrated, meaning harder to partition cleanly [14, 24].

### 5.2 Signed Cheeger Inequality

The signed Cheeger inequality relates the spectral measure to the combinatorial frustration: lambda_1(Delta_sigma) / 2 <= h_1_sigma <= sqrt(2 * lambda_1(Delta_sigma)), where h_1_sigma is the signed Cheeger constant relating to the minimum fraction of edges that need to be removed for balance [14]. The combinatorial frustration index (minimum edges to flip for balance) is NP-hard to compute, making the spectral bound invaluable as a practical proxy [15].

### 5.3 Normalized Measures

Three normalization options exist [14, 15]:
- f = lambda_min(L_sigma) / lambda_max(L_sigma) in the range 0 to 1 — scale-invariant
- f = lambda_min(L_sigma) / (2 * total_weight / n) — normalized by average degree
- f = lambda_min(L_norm) where L_norm = D_bar^{-1/2} L_sigma D_bar^{-1/2} — normalized signed Laplacian with eigenvalues in the range 0 to 2

### 5.4 Switching Invariance

The spectrum of the signed Laplacian is invariant under switching operations (flipping signs of all edges incident to a vertex subset) [14]. This means the frustration index captures intrinsic graph structure, not arbitrary sign conventions.

### 5.5 Computation Recipe

For the d x d Co-Information graph:
1. Construct W_signed = Co-Information matrix (negative = synergy, positive = redundancy)
2. D_bar = diag(sum(|W_signed|, axis=1))
3. L_sigma = D_bar - W_signed
4. evals = scipy.linalg.eigvalsh(L_sigma)
5. frustration = evals_min / evals_max (i.e., smallest eigenvalue divided by largest eigenvalue)

### 5.6 Interpretation for Hypothesis

LOW frustration (ratio < 0.1) means the graph is nearly balanced — clean synergistic/redundant modules exist, and oblique feature splits should help because features naturally group into coherent modules [14, 24]. HIGH frustration (ratio > 0.3) means no clean partition exists — features have mixed synergy/redundancy relationships that don't form clear modules, and standard axis-aligned methods may be equally effective [14, 15].

## 6. Choosing k (Number of Clusters)

### 6.1 Eigengap Heuristic

Sort the generalized eigenvalues lambda_1 <= lambda_2 <= ... and compute gaps Delta_i = lambda_{i+1} - lambda_i [18]. Choose k = argmax(Delta_i) [18]. The theoretical basis comes from perturbation theory: in the ideal case of k completely disconnected balanced subgraphs, eigenvalue 0 has multiplicity k with a gap to lambda_{k+1} > 0 [18]. The Davis-Kahan theorem bounds eigenvector perturbation inversely by the eigengap size [18]. This heuristic works well for well-separated clusters but can be ambiguous for overlapping or gradually varying community structure [18].

### 6.2 Silhouette on Eigenvector Embedding

After computing the n x k_max eigenvector embedding, sweep k from 2 to k_max and evaluate the silhouette score for each k [19]. The silhouette coefficient is defined as (b - a) / max(a, b), where a is the mean intra-cluster distance and b is the mean nearest-cluster distance, ranging from -1 to 1 with higher values indicating better-defined clusters [19].

### 6.3 Combined Heuristic

The recommended approach combines both methods: (1) compute eigenvalues for k_max = min(20, d//3) eigenvectors, (2) identify top-3 eigengap candidates, (3) for each candidate, compute silhouette score on k-means of the eigenvector embedding, (4) choose k with the best silhouette among top eigengap candidates [18, 19]. The fallback rule when the eigengap is ambiguous (max gap < 2 * median gap) is k = ceil(sqrt(d/2)) [18].

For Co-Information feature graphs, the practical range is k in {2, 3, ..., min(20, d/3)}, with typical values k=3-8 for d=200 features.

## 7. Co-Information Computation

### 7.1 Formula and Sign Interpretation

Co-Information (interaction information) for three variables is defined as [21, 22]:

  CoI(Xi, Xj; Y) = I(Xi; Y) + I(Xj; Y) - I(Xi, Xj; Y)

Positive CoI indicates redundancy — Xi and Xj provide overlapping information about Y, yielding a positive edge weight in the signed graph [21, 22]. Negative CoI indicates synergy — Xi and Xj together provide MORE information than the sum of their individual contributions, yielding a negative edge weight [21, 22]. Zero CoI means no interaction — Xi and Xj contribute independently to predicting Y [22].

### 7.2 Individual MI: I(Xi; Y)

Use sklearn.feature_selection.mutual_info_classif for classification targets or mutual_info_regression for regression targets [12]. These implement the KSG (Kraskov-Stoegbauer-Grassberger) k-nearest neighbor estimator [12, 17]. The KSG formula is: I(X;Y) = psi(k) + psi(N) - mean(psi(n_x + 1)) - mean(psi(n_y + 1)), where psi is the digamma function, k is the number of neighbors, N is the sample size, and n_x, n_y are neighbor counts within the Chebyshev distance in marginal spaces [13, 17]. The function returns an array of shape (d,) with the MI of each feature column with target Y, measured in nat units (natural logarithm base) [12]. Negative estimates are clipped to 0 since true MI >= 0 [12]. The default n_neighbors=3 but n_neighbors=5 is recommended for reduced variance [12]. Setting random_state ensures reproducibility, as sklearn adds 1e-10 noise to continuous features to break ties [12, 13].

### 7.3 Joint MI: I(Xi, Xj; Y) — CRITICAL IMPLEMENTATION DETAIL

sklearn's mutual_info_classif processes each column of X INDEPENDENTLY [12, 13]. Passing X[:, [i,j]] returns [I(Xi;Y), I(Xj;Y)], NOT the joint MI I((Xi,Xj); Y) [13]. A custom implementation is required for the joint MI.

Three approaches are viable:

**Approach 1 (Recommended): Custom KSG for 2D input.** Implement the Ross (2014) MI estimator for multivariate continuous X with discrete Y [13]. The algorithm naturally extends to 2D by using KDTree in 2D Chebyshev space. The implementation is approximately 20 lines: for each class in Y, find k-NN distances within that class using NearestNeighbors in 2D space, then count all neighbors within those distances using KDTree on the full 2D dataset, and apply the digamma formula [13].

**Approach 2: NPEET library.** The NPEET library supports multivariate inputs natively via list-of-lists format [16]. Usage: ee.mi(np.column_stack([X[:,i], X[:,j]]).tolist(), y.reshape(-1,1).tolist()) [16]. However, NPEET has known bias for strongly dependent variables and requires list-of-lists format rather than numpy arrays [16]. The NPEET_LNC variant provides Local Non-uniformity Correction for bias reduction [16].

**Approach 3: sklearn internal API.** Directly use sklearn's internal _compute_mi_cd with 2D input [13]. This relies on private API that may change between sklearn versions and is not recommended for production [13].

### 7.4 Full Computation Recipe

1. Compute individual MI: mi = mutual_info_classif(X, y, n_neighbors=5, random_state=42) [12]
2. For each pair (i,j): joint_mi_ij = joint_mi_cd(X[:, [i,j]], y, n_neighbors=5) using custom implementation [13]
3. CoI matrix: W[i,j] = mi[i] + mi[j] - joint_mi_ij [21, 22]
4. Diagonal: W[i,i] = 0 (no self-loops)
5. Symmetrize: W = (W + W.T) / 2 (should already be symmetric but floating point)
6. Parallelization: d*(d-1)/2 independent pair computations via joblib.Parallel with n_jobs=-1

### 7.5 KSG Estimator Bias Considerations

The KSG estimator has known negative bias, especially for strongly dependent variables and in higher dimensions [23]. For bivariate log-normal distributions, KSG is severely negatively biased even for N = 10,000 [23]. A bias-improved variant (BI-KSG) has been proposed that is proven consistent with better sample complexity for moderate N [23]. For our 2D joint MI estimation, this bias is mild but systematic, potentially leading to slight overestimation of synergy (more negative CoI values) [23]. Rank-based reparameterization of marginals before MI estimation can help remove bias in many cases [23].

## 8. Complete Implementation Pipeline (Pseudocode)

INPUT: Feature matrix X (n x d), target y (n,), task_type in {'classification', 'regression'}
OUTPUT: Feature modules {M_1, ..., M_k}, frustration_index f, cluster_labels

Step 1 — Individual MI: Use mutual_info_classif or mutual_info_regression with n_neighbors=5 [12].

Step 2 — Pairwise Joint MI: Compute joint_mi_cd(X[:,[i,j]], y) for all d*(d-1)/2 pairs using joblib parallelization [13].

Step 3 — CoI Matrix: W[i,j] = mi[i] + mi[j] - jmi[i,j]; set diagonal to 0 [21, 22].

Step 4 — Signed Decomposition: A_pos = max(W, 0) for redundancy edges; A_neg = max(-W, 0) for synergy edges [1].

Step 5 — Frustration Index: Construct L_sigma = D_bar - W; compute evals = eigvalsh(L_sigma); frustration = evals_min / evals_max (smallest over largest eigenvalue) [14, 24].

Step 6 — Degeneracy Check: If >95% of non-zero entries have same sign, fall back to unsigned spectral clustering [14].

Step 7 — Choose k: Compute k_max eigenvalues via eigh, apply eigengap heuristic to identify top-3 candidates, evaluate silhouette score for each, choose best [18, 19].

Step 8 — SPONGE Clustering: Solve (L_plus + tau_n * D_neg) w = lambda (L_neg + tau_p * D_pos) w via scipy.linalg.eigh for the k smallest eigenvectors; apply KMeans with n_init=20 [1, 9].

Step 9 — Extract Modules: M_j = {feature i : label[i] == j} [1].

## 9. Scalability Estimates

Co-Information computation dominates runtime at O(d^2 * n * log(n)) with KSG [12]. Each MI computation takes approximately 100ms for n=100K samples [12]. Eigendecomposition is O(d^3) for dense eigh but negligible for d <= 1000 [9]. Memory for the CoI matrix is d^2 * 8 bytes (8 MB for d=1000), presenting no concerns.

Estimated timings on 8 cores:
- d=50, n=10K: ~10 sec total (CoI ~10 sec, eigendecomp <0.1 sec)
- d=100, n=10K: ~40 sec total (CoI ~40 sec, eigendecomp <0.1 sec)
- d=200, n=50K: ~4 min total (CoI ~4 min, eigendecomp <0.5 sec)
- d=500, n=100K: ~26 min total (CoI ~26 min, eigendecomp ~5 sec)
- d=1000, n=100K: ~106 min total (CoI ~105 min, eigendecomp ~30 sec)

The d*(d-1)/2 pair computations are embarrassingly parallel, achieving near-linear speedup with joblib [12].

## 10. Pitfalls and Mitigations

**Pitfall 1: Zero-degree nodes in A_pos or A_neg (HIGH severity).** If a feature has no synergistic or no redundant relationships, the corresponding D_pos or D_neg row is zero, making the Laplacian singular and the generalized eigenvalue problem ill-defined [1]. Mitigation: The tau regularization parameters in SPONGE prevent this [1]. With tau_p, tau_n > 0, the matrices remain positive definite as long as each node has nonzero total degree [1]. For extra safety, add epsilon * I with epsilon=1e-10 [8, 9].

**Pitfall 2: All-positive or all-negative Co-Information graph (MEDIUM severity).** If >95% of CoI values have the same sign, signed clustering degenerates [14]. An all-positive graph collapses to standard unsigned clustering; an all-negative graph means all features are mutually synergistic [14, 24]. Mitigation: Check sign distribution before clustering and fall back to unsigned spectral clustering on |W| if heavily skewed.

**Pitfall 3: KSG estimator bias for strongly dependent variables (LOW-MEDIUM severity).** The KSG estimator has known negative bias for strongly dependent variables and in higher dimensions [23]. For 2D joint MI estimation, this bias systematically underestimates joint MI, leading to slight overestimation of synergy [23]. Mitigation: Use n_neighbors=5 instead of default 3, consider rank-based reparameterization, or use NPEET_LNC for bias correction [16, 23].

**Pitfall 4: LOBPCG convergence failure for small dense graphs (HIGH severity).** LOBPCG breaks when 5k > n and performs poorly for small matrices [10]. SigNet's default solver is LOBPCG, which is inappropriate for d=200-1000 feature graphs [6, 10]. Known convergence failures exist with provided initial guesses (scipy issue #10258) [20]. Mitigation: Bypass SigNet's solver entirely and use scipy.linalg.eigh for d <= 500 or scipy.sparse.linalg.eigsh with sigma=0 for d <= 1000 [9, 11].

**Pitfall 5: Sparse vs dense storage mismatch (LOW severity).** The Co-Information matrix is fully dense (every pair computed), but SigNet expects sparse CSC matrices [3, 6]. Converting a dense matrix to sparse wastes memory and adds overhead. Mitigation: Keep as dense numpy arrays and use scipy.linalg.eigh directly [9].

**Pitfall 6: Non-deterministic results (LOW severity).** Both MI estimation (noise addition) and k-means clustering use random initialization, causing results to vary between runs [12]. Mitigation: Set random_state in mutual_info_classif and KMeans; run k-means with n_init=20 for stability [12].

**Pitfall 7: Eigenvector normalization inconsistency (LOW-MEDIUM severity).** SigNet divides eigenvectors by eigenvalues (v = v / w) before k-means [6]. This weighting can amplify noise from eigenvectors with very small eigenvalues. Mitigation: Consider using raw eigenvectors row-normalized to unit length instead, or use SigNet's convention but clip eigenvalue weights to avoid division by near-zero values [6].

sources.title: Cucuringu et al. (2019) SPONGE: A generalized eigenproblem for clustering signed networks
sources.summary: Original SPONGE paper with complete mathematical formulations for the generalized eigenvalue problem, SPONGE_sym variant, regularization parameters, tau constraints, and theoretical guarantees under the Signed Stochastic Block Model.

sources.title: Cucuringu et al. (2022) Regularized spectral methods for clustering signed networks (JMLR)
sources.summary: Extended analysis with adaptive regularization parameters scaling as (np)^{6/7}, sparse regime guarantees, SSBM recovery conditions for general k >= 2, and evidence that SPONGEsym outperforms unnormalized SPONGE.

sources.title: SigNet Cluster API Documentation
sources.summary: Complete API reference for Cluster class including SPONGE, SPONGE_sym, and other methods with parameter signatures, defaults, and input format (tuple of sparse CSC matrices).

sources.title: SigNet Usage Documentation
sources.summary: Usage examples showing complete workflow: initialize Cluster from (A_pos, A_neg) tuple, call clustering methods, evaluate with adjusted_rand_score.

sources.title: SigNet GitHub Repository (archived Nov 4, 2025)
sources.summary: Repository archived Nov 4, 2025. Last significant commit Sept 2018. ~40 stars, ~10 forks. Install via pip from GitHub. Read-only status confirmed.

sources.title: SigNet cluster.py Source Code
sources.summary: Complete SPONGE implementation: constructs L_plus + tau_n*D_neg and L_neg + tau_p*D_plus, solves via lobpcg, divides eigenvectors by eigenvalues, applies KMeans. SPONGE_sym uses normalized Laplacians with identity regularization.

sources.title: SigNet setup.py
sources.summary: Version 0.1.0. Dependencies: scikit-learn, cvxpy (only for SDP_cluster), networkx (no version constraints). No Python version specified. Pre-installs numpy for ecos.

sources.title: SigNet utils.py Source Code
sources.summary: sqrtinvdiag uses safeguard dd=1/max(sqrt(x), 1/999999999) to prevent division by zero for zero-degree nodes. Same pattern in invdiag utility.

sources.title: scipy.linalg.eigh Documentation
sources.summary: Dense symmetric eigenvalue solver supporting generalized problem Aw=lambda*Bw. B must be positive DEFINITE. subset_by_index enables computing only k smallest. Returns (eigenvalues, eigenvectors) in ascending order.

sources.title: scipy.sparse.linalg.lobpcg Documentation
sources.summary: Iterative sparse eigensolver that BREAKS when 5k > n. Designed for extremely large n/k ratios. Solves 3k x 3k dense eigenproblems per iteration. Supports generalized problem via B parameter.

sources.title: scipy.sparse.linalg.eigsh Documentation
sources.summary: ARPACK-based sparse symmetric eigensolver. Shift-invert mode (sigma=0) efficiently finds smallest eigenvalues. Allows M (B matrix) to be positive semidefinite when sigma is specified.

sources.title: sklearn mutual_info_classif Documentation
sources.summary: KSG-based MI estimation between features and discrete target. Processes each feature column independently. n_neighbors=3 default. Returns MI in nat units, clips negatives to 0. Supports n_jobs for parallelization.

sources.title: sklearn _mutual_info.py Source Code
sources.summary: Internal KSG implementation: _compute_mi_cd uses NearestNeighbors with Chebyshev metric and KDTree for radius queries. Digamma formula: psi(n)+psi(k)-mean(psi(n_x+1))-mean(psi(n_y+1)). Adds 1e-10 noise to continuous features.

sources.title: Atay & Liu (2019) Cheeger constants, structural balance, and spectral clustering for signed graphs
sources.summary: Signed Cheeger inequality: lambda_1/2 <= h_1_sigma <= sqrt(2*lambda_1). Fundamental result: balance iff lambda_1=0. Switching invariance of spectrum. Multi-way extensions for k balanced components.

sources.title: Belardo et al. (2021) Inequalities for Laplacian Eigenvalues of Signed Graphs with Given Frustration Number
sources.summary: Bounds relating lambda_min to combinatorial frustration index. Computing frustration is NP-hard, making spectral lower bound invaluable as practical proxy.

sources.title: NPEET: Non-Parametric Entropy Estimation Toolbox
sources.summary: KSG-based entropy and MI estimation supporting multivariate inputs via list-of-lists format. Known bias for strong dependencies. NPEET_LNC variant provides Local Non-uniformity Correction.

sources.title: Kraskov et al. (2004) Estimating Mutual Information
sources.summary: Original KSG estimator paper. Two classes of k-NN based MI estimators that are data-efficient, adaptive, and have minimal bias. Uses Chebyshev distance for neighbor queries.

sources.title: Von Luxburg (2007) A Tutorial on Spectral Clustering
sources.summary: Comprehensive tutorial covering eigengap heuristic definition, Davis-Kahan perturbation theory justification, normalized vs unnormalized spectral clustering algorithms, and practical guidance.

sources.title: sklearn silhouette_score Documentation
sources.summary: Mean silhouette coefficient: (b-a)/max(a,b). Range [-1, 1]. Higher = better-defined clusters. Model-based evaluation method for choosing k.

sources.title: LOBPCG convergence failure (scipy #10258)
sources.summary: Known bug: LOBPCG fails with provided initial guesses, producing 'leading minor not positive definite' error. Workaround: use random initial vectors.

sources.title: dit library: Co-Information Documentation
sources.summary: Python library for discrete information theory with coinformation function. Co-Information defined via inclusion-exclusion over entropies. Positive = redundancy, negative = synergy.

sources.title: Wikipedia: Interaction Information (Co-Information)
sources.summary: Co-information can be positive (redundancy) or negative (synergy). For three variables: I(X;Y;Z) = I(X;Y) - I(X;Y|Z). Equivalent formulations via entropies and conditional MI.

sources.title: Gao et al. (2016) Demystifying Fixed k-NN Information Estimators
sources.summary: Bias-improved KSG (BI-KSG) estimator. KSG shown to have severe negative bias for log-normal distributions. BI-KSG proven consistent with better sample complexity for moderate N.

sources.title: Belardo & Simic (2014) Balancedness and the least eigenvalue of Laplacian of signed graphs
sources.summary: Fundamental result: signed graph is balanced iff lambda_min(L)=0. lambda_min serves as spectral proxy for balance deviation, with larger values indicating greater frustration.

follow_up_questions: Should we vendor SigNet's cluster.py (~200 lines) and utils.py (~50 lines) or reimplement the SPONGE algorithm from scratch using scipy.linalg.eigh? Vendoring preserves the exact SigNet convention (eigenvector/eigenvalue weighting) while reimplementation avoids the cvxpy dependency and LOBPCG limitation entirely.

Is the eigengap heuristic reliable for Co-Information feature graphs specifically? The Co-Information matrix may have a gradually varying spectrum rather than sharp gaps, especially when features have mixed synergy/redundancy patterns. Should we default to silhouette-based k selection instead?

What is the empirical accuracy of KSG-based Co-Information estimation vs binning-based methods for continuous features? The KSG estimator's known negative bias for strongly dependent variables could systematically underestimate joint MI, leading to overestimation of synergy (more negative CoI values). How significant is this effect for typical tabular datasets?
